title: Sharp nonuniqueness for the Navier-Stokes equations

def:1.1.summary: Weak solution of NSE on $T^d$: a function u $\in L^2_{t,x}$ satisfying the distributional formulation against divergence-free test functions in $C^\infty$ that vanish for t $\ge$ T. Allows general $L^2$ initial data attained distributionally.

def:1.2.summary: $X^{p,q}$ Serrin class: space of weak solutions in $L^p(0,T$; $L^q(T^d))$ with the Ladyzhenskaya-Prodi-Serrin scaling index 2/p + d/q $\le 1$. Subcritical when <1, critical when =1.

thm:1.3.summary: Ladyzhenskaya-Prodi-Serrin criteria: if u $\in X^{p,q}$ with 2/p + d/q $\le 1$, then u is the unique Leray-Hopf solution and is regular on (0,T]. Establishes the regularity side of the LPS threshold.

conjecture:1.4.summary: Sharp LPS Conjecture: for 2/p + d/q > 1 (strictly above the critical threshold), weak solutions in $X^{p,q}$ are non-unique and may be non-Leray-Hopf. The paper proves the p < 2, q $= \infty$ case.

thm:1.5.summary: Strong nonuniqueness in 2D: for any divergence-free $u_0 \in L^2(T^2)$, there exists a weak solution u different from the Leray-Hopf solution with $u(t) \to u_0$ strongly in $L^2$ as t $\to 0+$.

thm:1.6.summary: Sharp LPS nonuniqueness for d $\ge 2$: (1) any weak solution in $L^p_t L^\infty$ with p < 2 having an interval of regularity is non-unique; (2) non-Leray-Hopf solutions exist in $L^p_t L^\infty$ for all p < 2.

rem:1.7.summary: Explains that nonuniqueness does not stem from rough initial data but from persistent space-time oscillations. Notes that in d = 2,3,4 the Cauchy problem has infinitely many weak solutions for any $L^2$ initial data.

thm:1.8.summary: Main Theorem: for any p < 2, q $< \infty, \varepsilon > 0$, and smooth divergence-free v $\in C^\infty([0,T]\timesT^d)$, there exists a weak solution u $\in L^p_t L^\infty \cap L^1_t W^{1,q}$ that is $(1) \varepsilon-close$ to v in these norms, (2) smooth on a set I of intervals, (3) singular only on a set S $\subset [0,T]$ with Hausdorff dimension $\le \varepsilon$.

rem:1.9.summary: Notes u lies on the borderline of Beale-Kato-Majda $(L^1_t W^{1,\infty})$. The singular set S is nonempty; energy equality fails due to norm blow-up as singular times accumulate from intervals.

thm:1.10.summary: Euler analog: Theorem 1.8 holds verbatim for the Euler equations $(\nu=0)$, with the same space-time construction.

thm:1.11.summary: Anomalous dissipation: for Euler or NSE on $T^d (d \ge 2)$, there exist infinitely many non-energy-conserving weak solutions in $L^{3/2-\varepsilon}_t C^{1/3} \cap L^1_t C^{1-\varepsilon}$ with the same initial data. Lies at Onsager's $C^{1/3}$ threshold.

def:2.1.summary: NSE-Reynolds system: $\partial_t$ u $- \Deltau +$ div(uвЉ—$u) + \nablap =$ div R, where R is a symmetric trace-free stress tensor. A solution is well-prepared if R = 0 outside time intervals I at a prescribed timescale $\tau$.

prop:2.2.summary: Main Iteration: given any well-prepared (u,R) and $\delta > 0$, produces $(u_1, R_1)$ with вЂ–$R_1$вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le \delta$. The perturbation w $= u_1-u$ satisfies: support in I, вЂ–wвЂ–$_{L^2}$ в‰І вЂ–RвЂ–$^{1/2}_{L^1}$, вЂ–wвЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty \cap L^1_t W^{1,q}} \le \delta$.

prop:3.1.summary: Temporal concentration step: given well-prepared (u,R) at scale $\tau$Мѓ, constructs (Е«, RМ„) where RМ„ vanishes outside a strict subset I $\subset$ ДЁ at smaller scale $\tau < \tau$Мѓ/2, with вЂ–RМ„вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le$ CвЂ–RвЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r}$. Concentrates stress to isolated time intervals via gluing.

prop:3.2.summary: Sharp gluing: on each subinterval $[t_i, t_{i+1}]$, the unique short-time smooth NSE solution $v_i$ satisfies вЂ–$v_i$вЂ–$_{L^\infty H^d} \le \delta$ and вЂ–R $v_i$вЂ–$_{L^\infty L^r} \le C_r \int_{t_i}^{t_{i+1}}$ вЂ–R(t)вЂ–$_{L^r}$ dt $+ C_u \delta \tau^\varepsilon$. Enables gluing smooth local solutions to temporal support of stress.

prop:3.3.summary: Stress error from gluing: the glued solution (Е«, RМ„) satisfies вЂ–RМ„вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le C_r$ вЂ–RвЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r}$. Completes proof of prop:3.1 by controlling stress growth in the gluing procedure.

prop:4.1.summary: Convex integration step: given (Е«, RМ„) from prop:3.1 at set I and scale $\tau$, produces $(u_1, R_1)$ at the same I, $\tau$ with вЂ–$R_1$вЂ–$_{L^1 L^r} \le \delta$ and velocity perturbation w supported in I with вЂ–wвЂ–$_{L^2}$ в‰І вЂ–RМ„вЂ–^{1/2} and вЂ–wвЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty \cap L^1_t W^{1,q}} \le \delta$. Uses Mikado flows for spatial oscillation.

lem:4.2.summary: Geometric decomposition: for any compact N $\subset S^{d\timesd}_+$, there exist finite $\Lambda \subset Z^d$ and smooth $\Gamma_k$: N $\to$ R such that R $= \Sigma_{k\in\Lambda} \Gamma_k(R)^2 e_k$вЉ—$e_k$ for all R $\in$ N. Enables writing stress as sum of rank-1 matrices aligned with Mikado flow directions.

thm:4.3.summary: Stationary Mikado flows $W_k$: $T^d \to R^d$ are smooth divergence-free functions solving pressureless Euler $(div(W_k$вЉ—$W_k) = 0)$ with $\int_{T^d} W_k$вЉ—$W_k = e_k$вЉ—$e_k$ and $L^p$ estimates вЂ–$\nabla^m W_k$вЂ–$_{L^p}$ в‰І $\mu^{m+(d-1)/2-(d-1)/p}$. The building block for convex integration.

lem:4.4.summary: Amplitude coefficients $a_k$ are defined so that $\Sigma_{k\in\Lambda} a_k^2 \int W_k$вЉ—$W_k = \theta^2g_\kappa^2(\nut)\rho$ Id $- g_\kappa^2(\nut)R$М„, matching the stress term. They vanish for dist(t, $I^c) \le \tau$, ensuring correct temporal support.

lem:4.5.summary: Oscillation error structure: $R_osc = R_{osc,x} + R_{osc,t} + R_far$. The temporal part $w^{(t)}$ satisfies $\partial_t w^{(t)} + div(w^{(p)}$вЉ—$w^{(p)} +$ RМ„$) + \nablaP =$ div $R_osc$, where P involves an inverse-Laplacian term from the temporal stress derivative.

lem:4.6.summary: Reynolds closure: the new stress $R_1 = R_lin + R_cor + R_osc$ and new pressure $p_1 =$ pМ„ + P make $(u_1, R_1)$ a well-prepared solution of the NSE-Reynolds system with velocity perturbation support in I.

lem:5.1.summary: Parameter constraints: three inequalities relating $\nu, \kappa, \sigma, \mu, \lambda$ that must hold for large $\lambda^\gamma \in$ N. These encode the p < 2 condition and ensure all error terms in Sections 5.2-5.3 vanish as $\lambda \to \infty$.

lem:5.2.summary: Coefficient estimates: вЂ–$\partial_t^n \nabla^m a_k$вЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty} \le C_{u,m,n} (\nu\kappa)^n \kappa^{1/2-1/p}$. Controls derivatives of the amplitude coefficients needed for all subsequent error estimates.

prop:5.3.summary: Principal part estimates: вЂ–$w^{(p)}$вЂ–$_{L^2}$ в‰І вЂ–RМ„вЂ–$^{1/2}_{L^1} (from$ Mikado flow normalization + geometric lemma) and вЂ–$w^{(p)}$вЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty} +$ вЂ–$w^{(p)}$вЂ–$_{L^1_t W^{1,q}} \le C_u \lambda^{-\gamma} \to 0$.

prop:5.4.summary: Corrector $w^{(c)}$ estimates: вЂ–$w^{(c)}$вЂ–$_{L^2_t L^\infty} \le C_u \lambda^{-\gamma}$ and вЂ–$w^{(c)}$вЂ–$_{L^1_t W^{1,q}} \le C_u \lambda^{-\gamma}$. Uses antidivergence operator R and bilinear antidivergence B from Appendix B.

prop:5.5.summary: Temporal corrector $w^{(t)}$ estimates: вЂ–$w^{(t)}$вЂ–$_{L^\infty_t W^{1,\infty}} \le C_u \nu^{-1}$, and for large $\lambda$, вЂ–$w^{(t)}$вЂ–$_{L^2} \le$ вЂ–RМ„вЂ–^{1/2} and вЂ–$w^{(t)}$вЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty \cap L^1_t W^{1,q}} \le \delta/4$.

lem:5.6.summary: Linear error bound: вЂ–$R_lin$вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le \delta/4$ for large $\lambda$. $R_lin$ contains the viscous/transport terms from expanding the NSE in the perturbation w.

lem:5.7.summary: Correction error bound: вЂ–$R_cor$вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le \delta/4$ for large $\lambda$. $R_cor$ comes from the cross-term $w^{(p)}$ вЉ— $w^{(c)}$ and the corrector itself.

lem:5.8.summary: Oscillation error bound: вЂ–$R_osc$вЂ–$_{L^1_t L^r} \le \delta/4$ for large $\lambda$. Uses improved HГ¶lder inequality (lem:B.1) to handle oscillation in both x and t, and antidivergence (thm:B.3) for the off-diagonal terms.

thm:A.1.summary: $X^{p,q}$ at critical LPS threshold with d $\le$ q $\le \infty$ implies Leray-Hopf: proves the regularity side of conjecture:1.4 at the borderline 2/p + d/q = 1. Needed to show nonunique solutions genuinely violate Leray-Hopf structure.

thm:A.2.summary: Existence of weak solutions to the linearized NSE equation around u $\in X^{p,q}$ at critical threshold. Constructs Leray-Hopf solutions to the linearized system, used in the test function argument for thm:A.1.

thm:A.3.summary: For u $\in X^{p,q}$ at critical threshold with q > 2, any F $\in C^\infty_c([0,T]\timesT^d)$ yields a weak solution $\Phi \in L^\infty_t L^2 \cap L^2_t H^1$ to the adjoint linearized system that serves as a test function.

prop:A.4.summary: Sobolev trilinear estimate: for smooth u,v on $T^d, \int|u\cdot\nablav\cdot\Deltav|$ dx в‰І вЂ–uвЂ–$_{L^q}$ вЂ–$\nablav$вЂ–$_{L^2}^{(q-d)/q}$ вЂ–$\Deltav$вЂ–$_{L^2}^{(q+d)/q}$. Used in the energy inequality argument for thm:A.2.

lem:B.1.summary: Improved HГ¶lder inequality on $T^d$: |вЂ–$a\cdotf(\sigma\cdot)$вЂ–_p - вЂ–aвЂ–_p вЂ–fвЂ–_p| в‰І $\sigma^{-1/p}$ вЂ–aвЂ–$_{C^1}$ вЂ–fвЂ–_p. Quantifies how fast the oscillation of $f(\sigmax)$ decouples from the amplitude a(x) as $\sigma \to \infty$.

def:B.2.summary: Tensor-valued antidivergence operator R: assigns to any mean-zero vector field f a symmetric traceless matrix field Rf with div(Rf) = f. The operator satisfies вЂ–RfвЂ–$_{L^p}$ в‰І вЂ–fвЂ–$_{L^p}$.

thm:B.3.summary: Antidivergence R is $L^p$ bounded: вЂ–RfвЂ–$_{L^p}$ в‰І вЂ–fвЂ–$_{L^p}$ for any $1 \le$ p $\le \infty$. High-frequency version: вЂ–$Rf(\sigma\cdot)$вЂ–$_{L^p}$ в‰І $\sigma^{-1}$вЂ–fвЂ–$_{L^p}$, a crucial gain of $\sigma^{-1}$ from oscillation.

thm:B.4.summary: Bilinear antidivergence B: div(B(v,A)) = vA $- \int_{T^d}$ vA with вЂ–B(v,A)вЂ–$_{L^p}$ в‰І вЂ–vвЂ–$_{C^1}$ вЂ–RAвЂ–$_{L^p}$. Used to construct the divergence-free corrector $w^{(c)}$ from the quadratic interaction term.

edges.note: 2D strong nonuniqueness is the special case d=2 of the main theorem, applied with $u_0$ as initial data
Sharp LPS nonuniqueness follows by applying thm:1.8 to a given smooth solution v; non-Leray-Hopf solutions in $L^p_t L^\infty$ are produced directly
thm:A.1 establishes that Leray-Hopf solutions cannot live in $L^p_t L^\infty$ for p < 2 at the critical threshold, confirming the nonunique solutions are genuinely non-Leray-Hopf
Main theorem is proved by iterating prop:2.2 starting from a smooth solution, shrinking $\delta$ and $\varepsilon$ at each step, sending R $\to 0$
The proof formulates everything in the NSE-Reynolds system and the well-preparedness notion of def:2.1
Euler case $(\nu=0)$ uses the identical prop:2.2 iteration with $\nu=0$ in the NSE-Reynolds system; the parameter choices simplify
Anomalous dissipation follows from thm:1.8 applied to give solutions in $L^{3/2-\varepsilon}_t C^{1/3}$ by choosing p and q appropriately near the Onsager threshold
Step 1 of prop:2.2: prop:3.1 concentrates the stress to isolated time intervals I, producing a well-prepared pair (Е«, RМ„)
Step 2 of prop:2.2: prop:4.1 applies convex integration to (Е«, RМ„) to produce $(u_1, R_1)$ with small stress and controlled velocity perturbation
Sharp gluing (prop:3.2) provides the local smooth solutions $v_i$ that are glued together to concentrate the stress; estimates on $v_i$ feed into prop:3.3
prop:3.3 provides the stress error bound вЂ–RМ„вЂ– $\le$ CвЂ–RвЂ– for the glued solution, completing the proof of prop:3.1
prop:4.1 takes as input the (Е«, RМ„) produced by prop:3.1 with stress concentrated to I
Mikado flows $W_k$ are the fundamental building block for the perturbation $w^{(p)}$; their stress identity and $L^p$ estimates drive all subsequent estimates
Geometric lemma decomposes RМ„ into rank-1 terms aligned with Mikado directions, enabling amplitude coefficients $a_k$ to cancel the stress
Coefficient $a_k$ construction ensures $div(w^{(p)}$вЉ—$w^{(p)})$ cancels the main stress term RМ„ via Mikado averaging identity
Oscillation error structure lemma organizes $R_osc$ into spatial, temporal, and far-field parts needed for the estimates in Section 5
Reynolds closure confirms $(u_1, R_1)$ with $R_1 = R_lin + R_cor + R_osc$ satisfies the NSE-Reynolds system and well-preparedness conditions
Estimates on $w^{(p)}$ are needed to confirm the $L^2$ bound вЂ–wвЂ–$_{L^2}$ в‰І вЂ–RМ„вЂ–^{1/2} and the $L^p_t L^\infty$ smallness
Corrector $w^{(c)}$ estimates needed to close the $L^p_t L^\infty \cap L^1_t W^{1,q}$ bound on total perturbation w
Temporal corrector $w^{(t)}$ estimates needed to control the total perturbation and verify the support condition
Linear error $R_lin$ must be $\le \delta/4$ in $L^1_t L^r$ to achieve вЂ–$R_1$вЂ– $\le \delta$ in prop:4.1
Correction error $R_cor$ must be $\le \delta/4$ to close the Reynolds stress bound
Oscillation error $R_osc$ must be $\le \delta/4$; this is the most delicate estimate, requiring improved HГ¶lder and antidivergence
Coefficient $a_k$ defined via $\Gamma_k$ from the geometric lemma lem:4.2; the identity R $= \Sigma \Gamma_k^2 e_k$вЉ—$e_k$ makes the stress cancellation work
Parameter constraints in lem:5.1 ensure the $L^p$ exponents work out: condition (5.4) is precisely вЂ–$w^{(p)}$вЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty} \le \delta/4$
Coefficient estimates from lem:5.2 feed into the bound on $w^{(p)} = \Sigma a_k \theta g_\kappa W_k(\mu\cdot)$
Mikado flow $L^p$ estimates (part 2 of thm:4.3) directly give вЂ–$w^{(p)}$вЂ–$_{L^p_t L^\infty}$ via lem:5.1 constraints
Bilinear antidivergence B used to construct $w^{(c)}$ from the term $a_k g_\kappaB(\cdot, W_k$вЉ—$W_k)$, with $L^p$ estimate from thm:B.4
Antidivergence R used in the $W^{1,q}$ estimate for $w^{(c)}$ via вЂ–$Rf(\sigma\cdot)$вЂ– в‰І $\sigma^{-1}$вЂ–fвЂ–
The temporal corrector $w^{(t)}$ cancels $\partial_t a_k$ terms; its $L^\infty$ estimate uses the local smooth solution structure from sharp gluing (prop:3.2)
Linear error $R_lin$ contains antidivergence applied to the linear transport and viscous terms; $L^p$ bound uses thm:B.3

Improved HГ¶lder inequality (lem:B.1) applied to the spatial oscillation error $R_{osc,x}$: вЂ–$a_k W_k(\mu\cdot)$вЉ—$W_k(\mu\cdot)$вЂ– $\approx$ вЂ–$a_k$вЂ–$\cdot$вЂ–$W_k$вЉ—$W_k$вЂ– up to $\sigma^{-1/r}$ corrections
Antidivergence R applied to the oscillation error gives the $\sigma^{-1}$ gain needed to make $R_{osc}$ small
Proof of A.1 uses linearized solutions from thm:A.2 as test functions in the duality argument
thm:A.3 constructs the backward adjoint solution $\Phi$ that allows the energy argument in thm:A.1
Sobolev trilinear estimate prop:A.4 is used in the energy estimate for the linearized equation (thm:A.2)
Linearized NSE solutions are shown to satisfy the energy inequality in the sense of Definition 1.1 for the full NSE
The uniqueness/regularity part of thm:1.3 (LPS criteria) is established via thm:A.1 identifying critical $X^{p,q}$ solutions as Leray-Hopf, then applying standard regularity theory

layer_1_formulation.description: Definitions, background criteria, and statement of main results

layer_2_main_results.description: Main nonuniqueness theorems вЂ” LPS sharpness, Euler analog, anomalous dissipation

layer_3_iteration_structure.description: The main iteration proposition reducing the theorem to two sub-steps

layer_4_temporal_concentration.description: Gluing smooth local solutions to concentrate the Reynolds stress to isolated time intervals

layer_5_convex_integration.description: Space-time convex integration using Mikado flows to kill the Reynolds stress

layer_6_perturbation_estimates.description: Quantitative estimates on all three components of the velocity perturbation and all three parts of the new Reynolds stress

layer_7_lps_boundary.description: Appendix A: proving that critical LPS solutions are Leray-Hopf, justifying the sharpness claim

layer_8_technical_tools.description: Appendix B: antidivergence operators and improved HГ¶lder inequality used throughout

2112.03116.title: Non-uniqueness of Leray solutions of the forced Navier-Stokes equations

2004.09538.title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions for the transport equation at critical space regularity

1701.08678.title: Onsager's conjecture for admissible weak solutions
